content.text: When the model determines that multiple tools can be called at the same time — because their inputs are independent and neither result depends on the other — it returns all
blocks in a single response. Your application executes all of them and sends all results back in one user message. This cuts round-trip count and wall-clock latency.

attrs.alt: Parallel vs sequential tool call timing diagram
attrs.title: Sequential = 2 round-trips; Parallel = 1 round-trip

content.text: Parallel calls happen automatically — no configuration required.
The model decides when calls are independent and issues them together. Your job is to handle multiple tool_use blocks in one response and return all results in a single user message.
. The model generates them in one response because it determined the calls are independent:
Single call — model chose one tool
Parallel calls — model chose multiple independent tools
Model reasoned first, then decided to call
Even when the model issues parallel calls, you can execute them synchronously in a loop if latency is not critical. The key requirement is that all results go in a single user message:

Synchronous execution of parallel calls is simpler but does not capture the latency benefit. It is appropriate when tool calls are fast (< 100ms each) or when the bottleneck is the model's inference time, not tool execution.
For I/O-bound tools (HTTP requests, database queries),
runs all tool calls concurrently, letting them overlap their I/O waits:

starts all coroutines simultaneously. If both tools take 500ms, they finish in ~500ms total instead of ~1000ms sequential. The results arrive in the same order as the input list — no ID reordering needed.
For synchronous tool implementations that cannot easily be made async, a
provides parallelism without requiring async refactoring:
The thread pool is particularly effective when tools wrap third-party SDKs that do not support async. Set
to the number of tool calls (usually 2–5) — creating more threads than tools wastes resources.

The model does not issue parallel calls when one result depends on another. It sequences them across turns automatically. You do not need to detect or handle this differently — the same loop handles both:

# When one tool call depends on another's result, the calls are NOT parallel.
# The model detects dependencies and sequences them across separate turns.

# Example: user asks "Get order details and cancel it if it hasn't shipped"
# Turn 1: model calls get_order (needs result to decide whether to cancel)
# Turn 2: after seeing order is unshipped, model calls cancel_order

# Independent (safe to parallel):
# - "Weather in London and news about AI"         → two different data sources
# - "Stock price of AAPL and MSFT"                → two independent lookups
# - "Search for docs on auth AND docs on payments" → two separate searches

# Dependent (model WILL sequence these):
# - "Get the order, then cancel it"               → cancel needs the order ID
# - "Search for user, then update their email"    → update needs the user ID from search
# - "Get price, then place order if under $100"   → order depends on price condition

# You do NOT need to do anything special for dependent calls.
# The model handles sequencing automatically.
# Just ensure your loop handles any number of turns.
Independent calls (different data sources, no shared inputs) → model parallelises
Dependent calls (output of A is input to B) → model sequences across turns
Conditional calls (call B only if A's result satisfies condition) → model sequences
Your agent loop handles both cases identically — iterate content, execute tool_use blocks, return results
blocks in a single response, each block carries a unique
block in the next user message must echo that exact
. Because you control when results are gathered, you can return them in any order — Claude matches by ID, not by position.

import anthropic
import asyncio

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

async def call_tool(name: str, tool_input: dict) -> str:
    """Simulate an async tool call (replace with real logic)."""
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # I/O latency placeholder
    if name == "get_weather":
        return f"Weather in {tool_input['city']}: 22°C, partly cloudy"
    elif name == "get_stock_price":
        return f"Stock {tool_input['ticker']}: $142.50 (+1.2%)"
    return "unknown tool"

async def run_parallel_and_map(response) -> list[dict]:
    """Execute all tool_use blocks in parallel, return results keyed by ID."""
    tool_uses = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"]

    # Fire all calls concurrently — order of completion does NOT matter
    results = await asyncio.gather(*(
        call_tool(tu.name, tu.input) for tu in tool_uses
    ))

    # Map by tool_use_id (not list index)
    return [
        {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": tu.id, "content": result}
        for tu, result in zip(tool_uses, results)
    ]
— pair each result to its originating
field. If you rely on list position and your parallel execution returns out of order, results get silently swapped.
In a parallel batch, one tool can fail while others succeed. Never let a single failure abort the entire batch — the model needs results for every
for the failed call and normal content for the rest.

never raises (each coroutine catches its own exception), and the model receives a full set of results — it can then reason about which call failed and decide whether to retry, use partial data, or tell the user.
Parallelism only saves time if your tools are I/O-bound (network, disk) and take similar durations. Use a simple harness to confirm the speedup before committing to an async implementation:

import time, asyncio

async def timed_gather(tool_uses: list) -> tuple[list, float]:
    start = time.monotonic()
    results = await asyncio.gather(*(call_tool(tu.name, tu.input) for tu in tool_uses))
    elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
    return results, elapsed

def timed_sequential(tool_uses: list) -> tuple[list, float]:
    start = time.monotonic()
    results = [
        asyncio.run(call_tool(tu.name, tu.input)) for tu in tool_uses
    ]
    elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
    return results, elapsed

# Example with 4 tools each taking ~200 ms
# Sequential:  ~800 ms (sum of all latencies)
# Parallel:    ~200 ms (max of all latencies)
# Speedup:     ~4x for 4 independent calls
. For homogeneous calls the speedup is exactly
. For heterogeneous calls the slowest tool determines wall-clock time, so consider running very slow calls first or breaking them into smaller pieces.

is fine for a handful of tool calls. But if an agent can emit dozens of parallel calls (e.g. a bulk-search task), you need a semaphore to avoid hammering downstream APIs or exhausting connection pools:

import asyncio

MAX_CONCURRENT = 5  # respect external API rate limits

async def run_with_limit(tool_uses: list) -> list[dict]:
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(MAX_CONCURRENT)

    async def limited_call(tu):
        async with sem:
            return await safe_call_tool(tu)

    return await asyncio.gather(*(limited_call(tu) for tu in tool_uses))

, at most 5 tool calls execute simultaneously regardless of how many the model requested. The remaining calls wait in the semaphore queue. This keeps your agent well-behaved against APIs that enforce per-second rate limits.
for the first call, async out-of-order completion swaps results silently. Always use
raises on the first exception by default. Wrap each coroutine with
emitted by the model must have a corresponding
in the next user message. Missing results cause the API to return an error.
: If two tools modify the same resource (e.g.
), parallel execution causes race conditions. Design tool schemas to be stateless/idempotent wherever possible, or detect conflicts after gathering and return an error result.
Using asyncio.run inside an existing event loop
: In Jupyter notebooks or FastAPI handlers, an event loop is already running. Use
blocks when it determines calls are independent. Your loop handles them by iterating all
blocks, executing each tool, and returning all results in a single
message. For latency-sensitive applications, run tool executions concurrently with
(sync tools). Dependent calls are sequenced automatically by the model across separate turns.
